text: The surgeon begins a medial to lateral dissection in the left lower quadrant, where a phlegmon is tethering the tissue, providing stability for the procedure. The retroperitoneum appears uninvolved.

The surgeon elevates the superior hemorrhoidal vessel using a ligature and enters the retro-rectal space. The left ureter is identified, and the superior hemorrhoidal vessel is ligated. Attention is then directed to the phlegmon, which is significantly adherent to the left dome of the bladder and the anterior abdominal wall.

The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection to navigate through the retroperitoneal space, attempting to enter a natural plane more cephalad. The dissection is deeper than intended, prompting a correction to re-enter the proper retroperitoneal plane. The white line is taken down from the retroperitoneum and mesocolon, with careful attention to avoid retroperitoneal structures like the ureter. The camera switches to ICG mode to visualize the left ureter, aiming to identify it by its green fluorescence.

The surgeon navigates the chronic perforated cavity beneath the dome of the bladder, revealing the left adnexa drawn into the disease process. The round ligament on the left side is visible, and a left salpingo-oophorectomy is performed en bloc with the specimen. The left ureter is identified, highlighted by green fluorescence, facilitating the safe separation of the phlegmon.

The surgeon uses ICG to identify the ureter, ensuring a safe dissection. The attachments of the distal sigmoid and upper rectum to the lateral sidewall are taken down, freeing the structures. The proximal mesentery is dissected intracorporeally to facilitate a fan and steel extraction, ensuring the proximal conduit reaches the mid rectum for anastomosis. The distal mesentery is then addressed.

The surgeon navigates through the inflamed mesentery of the upper rectum, achieving hemostasis. Both proximal and distal mesenteric margins are taken, and ICG is used to confirm adequate blood supply. The distal section is divided in preparation for extracorporeal extraction through a four-centimeter fan and steel incision, where an anvil is placed.

The surgeon prepares for an EEA anastomosis using laparoscopic guidance to navigate the stapler through intestinal folds, ensuring the spike reaches the apex. The mesentery is checked for twisting and tension before completing the Baker side-to-end anastomosis. Compression is held briefly, and the stapler is withdrawn. A leak test is performed, confirming the anastomosis is patent, concluding the operation.
